info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use the TensorFlow.js Speech Commands model to recognize spoken words in real time and use them to move an emoji around the page. It's been trained to recognize 18 words including directions like "up", "down", "left", and "right".

info.title: libraries
info.text: We're importing three libraries. The first is the familiar nn library, then the core TensorFlow.js and lastly the speech-commands model which wraps TensorFlow with a microphone-based audio classifier.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We setup four global variables, model will hold the speech recognizer, config for the model settings, emoji is the element we'll move around the page, and button is the toggle control, we keep a reference to it so we can update its label.

info.title: the setup button
info.text: Rather than running immediately on page load, we create a button with the nn library and wire it to call setup on click. This matters because requesting microphone access, which happens inside setup, requires a user gesture in all modern browsers. The button provides that gesture.

info.title: loading feedback
info.text: The first thing setup does is call this.content('...loading...'). Here this refers to the button, because that's what called setup(). Updating the button's label right away gives the user immediate feedback that something is happening while the model downloads in the background.

info.title: model config
info.text: The config object sets how the model runs: the probability threshold (0.75) means we'll only act on predictions where the model is at least 75% confident, we avoid running our callback function for background noise, lastly our overlap factor controls how often the audio window is re-evaluated. We'll make use of this config object later when we run our model.

info.title: model loading
info.text: Then we call speechCommands.create() to create the model with 'BROWSER_FFT' which uses the browser's built-in Fast Fourier Transform for audio analysis. We await model.ensureModelLoaded() to download the model's weights locally.

info.title: emoji and position state
info.text: Once the model is loaded we create the emoji element and position it at (200, 200). We also store the current position in emoji.data.x and emoji.data.y. The .data object is a plain JavaScript object the nn library attaches to each element, giving us a convenient place to stash state without using separate global variables.

info.title: new button and hint
info.text: this.remove() deletes the setup button (this is the button, as before), then we create a new button for toggling the listener on and off, add a hint in italics about which words to say, and immediately call toggle() to start listening right away.

info.title: toggle function
info.text: The toggle function starts/stops the microphone listener. If the model is currently listening (tracked in the button's .listening data property), it calls model.stopListening() and updates the button's text. Otherwise it calls model.listen() which gets passed a callback function: heardSomething (the function to run everytime it hears something) and our config from earlier.

info.title: the callback
info.text: The heardSomething function is called by the model every time it classifies a new audio window. It receives a result object containing scores: an array of confidence values, one per word in the vocabulary. We also call model.wordLabels() to get the matching array of word strings.

info.title: finding the best match
info.text: We find the word with the highest confidence score using a simple loop, this pattern is sometimes called an "argmax". We track both the best score and its index, then use that index to look up the matching word name from the words array.

info.title: filtering results
info.text: Even though we set a threshold in the config, we also guard here with an explicit check: if the best score is below 0.75 we bail out early. We also skip 'background_noise' and 'unknown', these are special categories the model uses when it doesn't hear a recognized word.

info.title: moving the emoji
info.text: If a confident directional word was heard, we adjust emoji.data.x or emoji.data.y by 20 pixels in the corresponding direction, then call .position(x, y) to move the element. Storing the position in .data means we always have the current coordinates ready to update.

info.title: lots more
info.text: The Speech Commands model recognizes 18 words by default, not just directions but also numbers zero through nine and other short words. You could map each word to a different action, sound, or visual change to build a fully voice-driven interface.
